one_line_takeaway: Using an LLM not as a bug detector but as a scoring oracle that steers a fuzzer's power scheduler roughly doubles exploited-project detection (45->82) and coverage (1.1M->1.6M instructions) over ITYFUZZ while costing ~$0.15/project, at the price of occasional regressions when the LLM misreads a contract.
key_finding: Across 117 previously-exploited projects, LLM4FUZZ covered 1.6M instructions vs ITYFUZZ's 1.1M, and generated working exploits for 82 projects (avg 106s) vs ITYFUZZ's 45 projects (avg 259s). Scanning 600 audited, unexploited on-chain projects, it found 5 previously-unknown critical vulnerabilities affecting ~$247k in assets, all in under 30 minutes; Mythril and Echidna found none and ITYFUZZ found only 3 (one in 30 min, two taking days). Ablations show L-C (complexity) is usually fastest single producer but the full combination is consistently best.
core_contribution: LLM4FUZZ is a smart-contract fuzzing framework that uses an LLM to guide and prioritize an existing feedback-driven fuzzer's power scheduler, rather than asking the LLM to find bugs directly. Its own contribution (built on top of the ITYFUZZ fuzzer, which is cited prior/baseline work) is a producer/consumer architecture: four 'producers' query an LLM over a static-analysis-augmented, per-function hierarchical representation of a contract to emit numeric scores for (1) code-region complexity, (2) vulnerability likelihood, (3) interesting function-call sequence likelihood, and (4) invariant-dependency likelihood; 'consumers' then fold these scores into the fuzzer's per-test-case energy via defined formulas (Kcomplexity, Kvuln, Kseq, Kinvariant), capping total energy at 32x the fuzzer's original energy. The paper argues LLMs capture stateful dependencies, branch permissiveness, and semantic sequence relevance that cyclomatic-complexity/dataflow heuristics (used by cited baselines SMARTIAN and ITYFUZZ) miss.
threat_model: Defensive/pre-deployment auditing tool. The adversary is an attacker who exploits logic bugs in deployed Ethereum/EVM DeFi smart contracts (e.g., price-inflation, sandwich, and reentrancy-style attacks) to drain funds that are immutable and hold real assets. LLM4FUZZ is the defender's automated analyst: it aims to surface exploitable vulnerabilities (as concrete transaction-sequence exploits) faster and with higher coverage before contracts are deployed, and it also scans already-deployed contracts. It is not itself an attack tool, though the exploits it generates are attacker-usable.
limitations: (1) Not universally superior: on some projects (e.g., RES) LLM4FUZZ is slower than or fails where ITYFUZZ succeeds, traced to the LLM assigning low complexity/vulnerability scores to critical functions; the authors concede LLM misunderstanding is inevitable and suggest a costlier model like GPT-4. (2) No improvement on the Daedaluzz synthetic property-testing benchmark, because machine-generated puzzle invariants lack real semantics for the LLM to reason over. (3) Single, cheaper LLM (Llama 2 70B) used throughout; results may differ with stronger/weaker models. (4) Rescaling constants A/B were hand-tuned on a few campaigns. (5) Ground truth for 'vulnerability' relies on historically-exploited projects and chain forking; generalization to novel bug classes not directly measured. (6) Traditional-software extension (AFL++ on binutils) is a brief proof-of-concept, not a rigorous evaluation.

datasets_used: 117 previously-exploited real-world smart contract projects (LoC 100 to 100k) used for coverage and known-vulnerability evaluation
600 unexploited, audited on-chain projects across Binance Smart Chain, Base, Polygon, and Ethereum used for finding new vulnerabilities
15 randomly selected projects reported in the Table 1 detection-time comparison (e.g., NewFreeDAO, Axioma, FAPEN, AES, SEAMAN)
4 hand-crafted hard invariants (Uniswap V2 Flashloan, Uniswap V3 Flashloan, Team Finance, Sublime Pool Miscalculation) for invariant-violation timing
Daedaluzz benchmark for smart-contract property testing (found no significant improvement)
quantitative_results: Instruction coverage on 117 projects: 1.6M (LLM4FUZZ) vs 1.1M (ITYFUZZ baseline)
Exploits generated: 82 projects in avg 106s (LLM4FUZZ) vs 45 projects in avg 259s (ITYFUZZ)

5 new critical vulnerabilities found among 600 audited projects, totaling ~$247k in assets; LLM4FUZZ found all 5 in <30 min, ITYFUZZ found 3 (one in 30 min, two took days), Mythril and Echidna found 0
Invariant-violation times (ITYFUZZ -> LLM4FUZZ): Uniswap V2 Flashloan 459.1s -> 14.2s; Uniswap V3 Flashloan Timeout -> 92.0s; Team Finance 139.4s -> 6.5s; Sublime Pool Miscalculation 190.1s -> 58.8s

Table 1 example detection times: SEAMAN 1112.3s (baseline) -> 3.9s (LLM4FUZZ); ApeDAO 249.6s -> 2.1s; AES 6.6s -> 1.7s; several baseline-inf projects (NewFreeDAO, RFB, Sheep, BBOX, ARA) solved in finite time by LLM4FUZZ
Regression example: RES solved by baseline in 3.1s but LLM4FUZZ (and ablations L-C, L-V) fail to detect (inf)
One newly-found exploit required a >=28-transaction sequence, found in avg 1.9 hours by LLM4FUZZ while ITYFUZZ failed within 24 hours
Cost/time: Llama 2 70B, avg $0.15 per project, ~19s total for all LLM queries per project; LLM inference cost $1/1M tokens (Anyscale) to >$3/1M tokens (Replicate)
Energy cap set at 32x original fuzzer energy; complexity rescaling A=1.15, B=1200
baselines_compared: LLM4FUZZ ablations L-C (complexity only), L-V (vulnerability likelihood only), L-S (sequential likelihood only)
AFL++ vs AFL++ with LLM guidance (traditional-software extension)
